description: HLLQP - LIFE 07 QZ - Taxation of Life Insurance and Tax Strategies - Section 1
examTips: Focus on death benefit tax treatment, policy dispositions, ACB calculations, and policy classification rules.

questions.question: Under the Income Tax Act, how is the death benefit of a personally owned life insurance policy treated for tax purposes?
options: A) It is fully taxable to the beneficiary.
B) Half of it is taxed as a capital gain.
C) It is entirely exempt from income tax.
D) It is taxable only if it exceeds $10,000.
optionFeedback: Death benefits from personally owned policies are not fully taxable.
Death benefits do not receive capital gains treatment.
It is entirely exempt from income tax.
There is no dollar threshold for exemption.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question asks (tax treatment of death benefit).
Step 2: Apply Income Tax Act rules for personally owned life insurance.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using facts. Death benefits are not taxable unless they relate to investment income earned within the policy.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: It is entirely exempt from income tax.

questions.question: Which of the following events would result in a "disposition" of a life insurance policy?
options: A) Changing the beneficiary.
B) Paying the annual premium.
C) Surrendering the policy for its cash value.
D) Changing the address of the policyholder.
optionFeedback: Changing the beneficiary does not constitute a disposition.
Paying premiums does not constitute a disposition.
Surrendering the policy for its cash value.
Changing address is an administrative change, not a disposition.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what constitutes a disposition of a policy.
Step 2: Apply the definition: a disposition occurs when you give up ownership/control and receive value.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using facts. Beneficiary and address changes are administrative; premium payments are not dispositions.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Surrendering the policy for its cash value.

questions.question: How is a "policy gain" calculated when a life insurance policy is fully surrendered?
options: A) Proceeds of disposition minus the Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB).
B) Total premiums paid minus the death benefit.
C) Cash surrender value plus the death benefit.
D) The total premiums paid multiplied by the inflation rate.
optionFeedback: Proceeds of disposition minus the Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB).
This calculation does not determine policy gain.
This ignores the cost basis.
Inflation is not a factor in policy gain calculation.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question asks (policy gain calculation).
Step 2: Apply the formula: Policy Gain = Proceeds of Disposition - ACB.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Total premiums minus death benefit is not relevant; inflation multiplier is incorrect.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: Proceeds of disposition minus the Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB).

questions.question: What does the Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB) of an insurance policy generally represent?
options: A) The total death benefit payable.
B) The future cash value of the policy.
C) The cost of the policy for tax purposes.
D) The commission paid to the insurance agent.
optionFeedback: ACB is not the death benefit.
ACB is not a projection of future value.
The cost of the policy for tax purposes.
Agent commission is a one-time fee, not the ACB.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Define Adjusted Cost Basis.
Step 2: Apply the concept: ACB represents cumulative premiums and other costs minus dividends received.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. ACB is not the death benefit, future value projection, or agent fees.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: The cost of the policy for tax purposes.

questions.question: Life insurance policies are classified into different groups for tax purposes. Which group represents "grandfathered" policies last acquired before December 2, 1982?
options: A) G1 policies
B) G2 policies
C) G3 policies
D) G4 policies
optionFeedback: G1 policies are grandfathered (pre-December 2, 1982).
G2 policies are post-1982.
G3 policies are post-1982 with revised rules.
G4 policies are the newest classification.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify policy classifications.
Step 2: Apply the timeline: G1 = pre-December 2, 1982 (grandfathered); G2/G3 = post-1982.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. G2, G3, and G4 are all post-1982 classifications.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: G1 policies.

questions.question: If a policyholder chooses to receive their participating policy dividends in cash, how does this affect the policy?
options: A) It increases the death benefit.
B) It reduces the policy's Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB).
C) It triggers an immediate 50% tax penalty.
D) It converts the policy into term insurance.
optionFeedback: Receiving dividends in cash does not increase the death benefit.
It reduces the policy's Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB).
There is no automatic tax penalty for receiving dividends.
Cash dividends do not convert policy type.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the effect of taking dividends in cash.
Step 2: Apply the rule: Dividends received reduce the ACB of the policy.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Dividends do not increase death benefit, trigger penalties, or convert policy type.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: It reduces the policy's Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB).

questions.question: When calculating the proceeds of disposition for a full surrender, what is deducted from the Cash Surrender Value (CSV)?
options: A) The agent's original commission.
B) Outstanding policy loans and unpaid premiums.
C) The Net Cost of Pure Insurance (NCPI).
D) The total amount of premiums paid.
optionFeedback: Agent commission is not deducted from proceeds.
Outstanding policy loans and unpaid premiums.
NCPI is a separate component, not deducted from CSV.
Premiums are already reflected in CSV calculation.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify deductions from CSV in a surrender.
Step 2: Apply the rule: Proceeds of Disposition = CSV - Outstanding Loans - Unpaid Premiums.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Agent commissions are not deducted; NCPI doesn't reduce CSV; premiums are already accounted for.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: Outstanding policy loans and unpaid premiums.

questions.question: If a policyholder reduces their life insurance coverage amount from $200,000 to $150,000, how is this treated for tax purposes?
options: A) It is ignored until death.
B) It is treated as a full surrender of the entire policy.
C) It is treated as a partial surrender, and the ACB is prorated.
D) It triggers a tax on the entire cash surrender value.
optionFeedback: Coverage reductions have tax implications.
Partial changes are not full surrenders.
It is treated as a partial surrender, and the ACB is prorated.
Tax applies only to the portion reduced, not the entire CSV.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the type of disposition (partial).
Step 2: Apply partial surrender rules: ACB is allocated proportionately to the reduced coverage.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Reductions are not ignored; they're not full surrenders; tax applies to the portion withdrawn.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: It is treated as a partial surrender, and the ACB is prorated.

questions.question: For a G2 or G3 universal life policy, what happens when a policyholder withdraws cash from the investment account?
options: A) The withdrawal is totally tax-free in all situations.
B) A prorated portion of the policy's ACB is used to calculate the policy gain.
C) The policy is automatically canceled.
D) The insurance company must double the death benefit.
optionFeedback: Withdrawals may trigger taxable gains.
A prorated portion of the policy's ACB is used to calculate the policy gain.
Withdrawals do not automatically cancel the policy.
The insurance company does not automatically increase benefits.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the withdrawal type (partial from UL policy).
Step 2: Apply G2/G3 rules: Use prorated ACB to determine policy gain on the withdrawal amount.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Withdrawals can be taxable; policy doesn't auto-cancel; death benefit doesn't auto-increase.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: A prorated portion of the policy's ACB is used to calculate the policy gain.

questions.question: Under what condition will taking a policy loan trigger a taxable policy gain?
options: A) If the loan is taken before the life insured turns 18.
B) If the loan amount is greater than the policy's Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB).
C) If the loan is used to buy a car.
D) All policy loans are fully taxable immediately.
optionFeedback: Age of the insured is not relevant.
If the loan amount is greater than the policy's Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB).
Use of funds does not determine tax treatment.
Loans are not automatically taxable.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify when a policy loan triggers tax.
Step 2: Apply the rule: If loan amount > ACB, the excess is a taxable policy gain.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Age is irrelevant; use of funds matters for deductibility, not taxability; loans are not automatically taxable.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: If the loan amount is greater than the policy's Adjusted Cost Basis (ACB).
